title: Create tutorial-01.md file
body:
This file contains a basic introductory tutorial on buildah. It covers 
buildah from a base
briefly describes containers/storage and containers/image
buildah from scratch
installing packages and files to a scratch image 
buildah push and running a buildah built container in docker
buildah bud

comments.body: @ipbabble this is some serious awesome-sauce!  I just took a quick scan, it looks great overall.  I spotted a couple of nits, will go through in more detail later today.  We should spin one of these up for the kpod tools too, perhaps skopeo.
Lets turn it into a blog as well.

Thanks for all the feedback. I forgot the DCO on the commit so I have to
recommit before this PR will be acceptable. I'll make these changes before
I do the commit and PR.

William

William Henry
Senior Consulting Software Engineer
Red Hat
Colorado, USA.

On Fri, Oct 27, 2017 at 8:14 AM, Nalin Dahyabhai <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> *@nalind* commented on this pull request.
> ------------------------------
>
> In docs/tutorial-01.md
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/buildah/pull/300#discussion_r147422201>:
>
> > +
> +Let's try running something else:
> +
> +    buildah run $container java
> +
> +Oops. Java is not installed. A message containing something like the following was returned.
> +
> +    container_linux.go:274: starting container process caused "exec: \"java\": executable file not found in $PATH"
> +
> +Lets try installing it using:
> +
> +    buildah run $container -- dnf install java
> +
> +The `--` syntax basically says: no more `buildah run` command options after this point. It is required if the command we specify includes command line options which are not meant for buildah.
> +
> +Now running `java` will show that Java has been installed. It will return the `Usage`:
>
> I think either "`java` in the container" or "`buildah run java`" would be
> clearer here.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/buildah/pull/300#pullrequestreview-72518448>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/ABPpgf5UWlFlclQTNv38yd8cBFxpPDNMks5sweUsgaJpZM4QIgvA>
> .
>

Nalin,

"scratch" absolutely shows up in the containers list. It shows up as the
IMAGE NAME of the working-container.

ac8fa6be0f0a     *                  scratch
working-container


William

William Henry
Senior Consulting Software Engineer
Red Hat
Colorado, USA.

On Fri, Oct 27, 2017 at 8:19 AM, Nalin Dahyabhai <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> *@nalind* commented on this pull request.
> ------------------------------
>
> In docs/tutorial-01.md
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/buildah/pull/300#discussion_r147423563>:
>
> > +
> +One of the advantages of using `buildah` to build OCI compliant container images is that you can easily build a container image from scratch and therefore exclude unnecessary packages from your image. E.g. most final container images for production probably don't need a package manager like `dnf`.
> +
> +Let's build a container from scratch. The special "image" name scratch tells buildah to create an empty container:
> +
> +    newcontainer=$(buildah from scratch)
> +
> +You can see this new empty container by running:
> +
> +    buildah containers
> +
> +Its container name is working-container by default. And it's stored in the `$newcontainer` variable. Notice the image name is scratch. And when we run:
> +
> +    buildah images
> +
> +We don't see it listed. That is because "scratch" is not an image. It is an empty container.
>
> "scratch" won't show up in the containers list, either, and commands that
> deal with containers won't see it. It's just a name that we specifically
> recognize as meaning "no image". I'm harping on this, probably a little too
> much, because I think it's very important that we are always clear about
> whether we're talking about containers or images, so that readers don't get
> confused.
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/projectatomic/buildah/pull/300#pullrequestreview-72520094>,
> or mute the thread
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/ABPpgWKNiqtMnDv3zJaQR9RtDnC2PlJyks5sweZlgaJpZM4QIgvA>
> .
>

You're right, the placeholder value shows up as the name of the image which was used to create the container, so that we always have a value there.  The wording suggested to me that we'd expect to see a container named "scratch" in the list, so maybe rewording it would make that clearer.

Can you sign the commits?  There's a longer explanation in the new CONTRIBUTING.md, but it boils down to adding a "Signed-off-by: William Henry <emailaddress>" to the commit log messages for each patch in the PR.  That looks like the reason the CI tests aren't passing.
This is a good read.  Thanks for putting it together.
I guess I close this one and then backout my commit on my fork and start over with the DCO and a new PR. Right?
Assuming you've gitconfig setup with your username, I think you can just do changes, "git add {files}", "git commit --amend -s" and then push it over again.

With multiple commits, 'git rebase -i \<upstreamreponame\>/master', then replacing 'pick' with 'reword' in the list of commits that it initially offers will let you edit the commit logs for each patch in turn, which should let you append the Signed-off-by: that the validation test expects.
